audit_status.source_base: Greek 1 Timothy text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact draft reviewed for prayer for all, one mediator, modesty, women learning/teaching passage, Adam/Eve argument. Requires second-pass source, syntax, and reception-history audit.

verses.restored: First of all, then, I appeal that petitions, prayers, intercessions, thanksgivings be made for all humans,
for kings and all those being in high position, so that we may lead a tranquil and quiet life in all reverence and dignity.
This is beautiful/good and acceptable before Divine our Savior/Healer,
who wills all humans to be saved/healed and to come into knowledge of truth.
For there is one Divine and one mediator of Divine and humans, human Messiah Jesus,
the One giving himself as ransom/release-price for all, the witness in its own seasons.
Into this I was appointed herald and apostle, I speak truth, I do not lie, teacher of nations in trust and truth.
Therefore I desire the men in every place to pray, lifting holy hands without anger and argument.
Likewise also women, in respectable arrangement, with modesty and soundness of mind, to adorn themselves not with braided hair and gold or pearls or costly clothing,
but with what is fitting for women professing reverence for the Divine: through good works.
Let a woman learn in quietness, in all teachability/orderly submission.
But I do not permit a woman to teach or to domineer over a man, but to be in quietness.
For Adam was formed first, then Eve.
And Adam was not deceived, but the woman, having been deceived, has come into transgression.
But she will be saved/healed through the childbearing, if they remain in trust and love and holiness with soundness of mind.

safety_note: 1 Timothy 2:11-15 is variant-sensitive in interpretation and highly dangerous in reception history. The draft preserves the text while noting that authentein is rare and can mean domineer/usurp rather than ordinary teaching authority. This passage must not be used to silence women broadly without second-pass Greek syntax, historical-context, and reception-history audit, especially because women pray/prophesy and serve as coworkers elsewhere in the Pauline corpus.
